grammarLessons.name: Equational Sentences — No Verb To Be

cards.grammarNote:
Arabic adjectives agree in gender: **سعيد** (sa'id, m.) / **سعيدة** (sa'ida, f.).
They also agree in definiteness when modifying a noun:
**رجل سعيد** (a happy man) vs. **الرجل السعيد** (the happy man).
In predicate position ('I am happy'), the adjective stays indefinite: **أنا سعيد** (ana sa'id).

**حَزِين** (ḥazīn, sad) — **فَعِيل** adjective pattern from root **ح-ز-ن** (ḥ-z-n, to be sad). **حَزِنَ** (ḥazina, to be sad), **حُزن** (ḥuzn, sadness — noun), **حَزَن** (ḥazan, grief). The **فَعِيل** pattern often describes a lasting state or quality.
Feminine: **حَزِينة** (ḥazīna). Plural: **حُزَنَاء** (ḥuzanāʾ — broken plural **فُعَلاء**).

**حُبّ** (ḥubb, love) from root **ح-ب-ب** (ḥ-b-b, to love). **أَحَبَّ** (aḥabba, to love — Form IV), **حَبِيب** (ḥabīb, beloved/darling — **فَعِيل** pattern), **مَحبُوب** (maḥbūb, loved one — passive participle), **مَحَبَّة** (maḥabba, affection/love).
**أَشعُر بِحُبٍّ** = I feel love — note the **بـ** (bi-) preposition: شَعَرَ بـ = to feel [noun].
cards.englishPhrase: I feel a lot of love for my family.

cards.grammarNote:
**غَضَب** (ghaḍab, anger) from root **غ-ض-ب** (gh-ḍ-b, to be angry). **غَضِبَ** (ghaḍiba, to become angry — stative Form I), **غَاضِب** (ghāḍib, angry — **فَاعِل** active participle), **غَضبَان** (ghaḍbān, furious — **فَعلَان** intensive adjective for emotional states).
**شَعَرتُ بِالغَضَب** = I felt anger — definite noun after بـ.
cards.englishPhrase: I felt anger when they lied to me.

cards.grammarNote:
**خَوف** (khawf, fear) from root **خ-و-ف** (kh-w-f, to fear). **خَافَ** (khāfa, to be afraid — Form I with a middle weak root), **خَائِف** (khāʾif, afraid — **فَاعِل**), **خَوَّافة** (khawwāfa, cowardly — intensive **فَعَّال**).
**أَنَا خَائِف مِن الظَّلَام** — note the definite article on **الظَّلَام** (aẓ-ẓalām, the dark) — sun letter **ظ**.

**أَمَل** (amal, hope) from root **أ-م-ل** (ʾ-m-l, to hope/expect). **أَمَّلَ** (ammala, to give hope — Form II), **تَأَمَّلَ** (taʾammala, to contemplate/meditate — Form V), **مُؤَمِّل** (muʾammil, one who hopes).
**المُستَقبَل** (al-mustaqbal, the future) from root **ق-ب-ل** (q-b-l, to receive/accept) — Form X: 'the time that will be received ahead.'
cards.englishPhrase: I have hope for the future.

cards.grammarNote: **ضَغط** (ḍaghṭ, pressure/stress) from root **ض-غ-ط** (ḍ-gh-ṭ, to press/squeeze). **ضَغَطَ** (ḍaghaṭa, to press), **ضَغط الدَّم** (ḍaghṭ ad-dam, blood pressure). In Modern Arabic, **ضَغط** covers both physical pressure and psychological stress/pressure at work.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of stress at work.

cards.grammarNote:
**مِزَاج** (mizāj, mood/temperament) from root **م-ز-ج** (m-z-j, to mix/blend). In classical medicine, *mizāj* referred to one's bodily constitution — the blend of the four humors. The medieval Arabic medical tradition (Ibn Sina/Avicenna) used this concept; European medicine adopted it as 'temperament.'
**مِزَاجِي جَيِّد** = my mood is good (equational sentence — no verb 'to be').
cards.englishPhrase: I'm in a good mood today.

cards.grammarNote:
**شُعُور** (shuʿūr, feeling/awareness) — verbal noun from root **ش-ع-ر** (sh-ʿ-r, to feel/perceive/know). **شَعَرَ** (shaʿara, to feel), **شِعر** (shiʿr, poetry — originally 'to be aware of beauty'), **شَاعِر** (shāʿir, poet — **فَاعِل**). The link between feeling and poetry is embedded in the root.
**طَبِيعِيّ** (ṭabīʿī, natural/normal) from root **ط-ب-ع** (ṭ-b-ʿ, nature/print).

**عَاطِفة** (ʿāṭifa, emotion/feeling/sentiment) — **فَاعِلة** feminine active participle from root **ع-ط-ف** (ʿ-ṭ-f, to bend toward/show affection). Plural: **عَوَاطِف** (ʿawāṭif — broken plural **فَوَاعِل**).
**لَم أَستَطِع** (lam astaṭiʿ, I could not) — **لَم** negates the past with a present-tense verb (jussive mood). Root **ط-و-ع** (ṭ-w-ʿ, to obey/be able), Form X.

**هُدُوء** (hudūʾ, calm/tranquility) from root **ه-د-أ** (h-d-ʾ, to calm down/be still). **هَدَأَ** (hadaʾa, to calm down), **هَادِئ** (hādiʾ, calm — **فَاعِل**), **تَهدِئَة** (tahdiʾa, calming/pacification — Form II verbal noun).
Note: **هُدُوئِك** (hudūʾik) = your calm — possessive suffix **كِ** (ki, your, f.) attached to the noun.

**سَلَام** (salām, peace/safety/greeting) from root **س-ل-م** (s-l-m, to be safe/submit). One of the most important roots in Arabic: **إِسلَام** (Islam, submission/peace), **مُسلِم** (Muslim, one who submits), **سَلَّمَ** (sallama, to greet/hand over), **سَلَامة** (salāma, safety).
The greeting **السَّلَام عَلَيكُم** (as-salāmu ʿalaykum) = peace be upon you.

**قَلَق** (qalaq, anxiety/worry) from root **ق-ل-ق** (q-l-q, to be restless/agitated). Reduplicated root. **قَلِقَ** (qaliqa, to be anxious), **قَلِق** (qaliq, anxious/worried — **فَعِل** stative pattern). In Modern Arabic **قَلَق** is widely used for both worry and existential anxiety.
Note: this is both the noun **قَلَق** and the adjective **قَلِق** — different vowels, same root.

**مُفَاجَأة** (mufājaʾa, surprise) — Form III verbal noun from root **ف-ج-أ** (f-j-ʾ, to come suddenly). **فَجَأَةً** (fajʾatan, suddenly — adverb), **فَاجَأَ** (fājaʾa, to surprise/take by surprise). Plural: **مُفَاجَآت** (mufājaʾāt, surprises — sound feminine plural).
**يَا لَهُ مِن مُفَاجَأَة!** = what a surprise! — the Arabic exclamation formula (see: يا له من...).

**شَغَف** (shaghaf, passion/infatuation) from root **ش-غ-ف** (sh-gh-f, to pierce the heart/become infatuated). Classically it referred to the pericardium (the membrane around the heart) — passion that 'reaches the heart.' **شَغَفَهُ حُبّاً** = love consumed him (literally 'reached his heart membrane').
**بِشَغَف** (bi-shaghaf) = with passion — the **بـ** preposition creates an adverbial phrase.

**رَغبَة** (raghba, desire/wish) from root **ر-غ-ب** (r-gh-b, to desire/want). **رَغِبَ فِي** (raghiba fī, to desire/want something), **رَاغِب** (rāghib, desirous — **فَاعِل**). Note: **رَغِبَ عَن** (raghiba ʿan) means the opposite — 'to have no desire for / reject.'
**قَوِيَّة** (qawiyya, strong, feminine) from root **ق-و-ي** (q-w-y, to be strong).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a strong desire to travel.

cards.grammarNote:
**كُره** (kurh, hate/hatred) from root **ك-ر-ه** (k-r-h, to hate/dislike). **كَرِهَ** (kariha, to hate — Form I stative), **كَرَاهِيَة** (karāhiyya, hatred — a longer verbal noun), **مَكرُوه** (makrūh, disliked — passive participle, also a religious term for a discouraged but not forbidden act).
Note the short form **كُره** (kurh) vs. the longer noun **كَرَاهِيَة** (karāhiyya) — both mean hatred.

**مَتعَة** (matʿa, pleasure/enjoyment) from root **م-ت-ع** (m-t-ʿ, to enjoy/provide). **اِستَمتَعَ** (istamtaʿa, to enjoy — Form X), **مُتعَة** (mutʿa, enjoyment), **يَسُرُّني** (yasurruni, it pleases me) uses a different root **س-ر-ر** (s-r-r, to delight). Both can express 'pleasure to meet you.'
**مُقَابَلتُكَ** (muqābalatuka) = meeting you — possessive suffix **كَ** on verbal noun.
cards.englishPhrase: It's a pleasure to meet you.

cards.grammarNote:
**أَلَم** (alam, pain) from root **أ-ل-م** (ʾ-l-m, to hurt/pain). **أَلِمَ** (alima, to be in pain), **أَلِيم** (alīm, painful — **فَعِيل**), **مُؤلِم** (muʾlim, causing pain — Form IV active participle), **مُؤلِمة** (muʾlima, painful, feminine).
**سَاقِي** (sāqī) = my leg — possessive suffix **ي** (-ī) on **سَاق** (sāq, leg).
cards.englishPhrase: I feel pain in my leg.

cards.grammarNote:
**طَاقَة** (ṭāqa, energy/capacity) from root **ط-و-ق** (ṭ-w-q, to be able/to encircle). **أَطَاقَ** (aṭāqa, to be capable of — Form IV), **طَاقَة** literally means 'capability/capacity,' extended in Modern Arabic to physical and electrical energy.
**لَدَيَّ طَاقَة كَثِيرة** — indefinite noun in subject position of equational sentence after لدي.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of energy today.

cards.grammarNote:
**اِرتِيَاح** (irtiyāḥ, relief/comfort) — Form VIII verbal noun from root **ر-و-ح** (r-w-ḥ, to rest/breathe). **اِرتَاحَ** (irtāḥa, to feel relieved), **رَاحَة** (rāḥa, rest/comfort), **رُوح** (rūḥ, spirit/soul — literally 'breath of life'), **مُرتَاح** (murtāḥ, at ease — Form VIII active participle).
All share the sense of 'breathing freely' — rest, spirit, and relief.

**عَزَاء** (ʿazāʾ, consolation/comfort) from root **ع-ز-ي** (ʿ-z-y, to console). **عَزَّى** (ʿazzā, to console/offer condolences — Form II), **تَعزِيَة** (taʿziya, condolences — Form II verbal noun). **عَزَاء** specifically refers to the comfort offered at a time of loss — it is especially used for condolence visits.
**عَزَاءً** ends in tanwin **ـاً** as the predicate of **كَانَ** (indefinite accusative).

**يَشعُر** (yashʿur, he feels) from root **ش-ع-ر** (sh-ʿ-r, to perceive/feel). Always used with **بـ** (bi-) when expressing a feeling: **شَعَرَ بِـ** = to feel [noun].
**أَشعُر بِخَير** (ashʿur bi-khayr) = I feel well/good. **خَير** (khayr, good/well-being) from root **خ-ي-ر** (kh-y-r, good). Note: you cannot say *أشعر خيراً* — the بـ is obligatory.

**يُحِبّ** (yuḥibb, he loves) — Form IV of root **ح-ب-ب** (ḥ-b-b, to love). Form IV (**أَفعَلَ**) here is the active form. **أَحَبَّ** (aḥabba, to love), **حَبِيب** (ḥabīb, beloved — **فَعِيل**), **حُبّ** (ḥubb, love — noun).
**أُحِبُّكَ** (uḥibbuka) = I love you (m.) / **أُحِبُّكِ** (uḥibbuki) = I love you (f.) — gender matters in the suffix.

**يَكرَه** (yakrahu, he hates) from root **ك-ر-ه** (k-r-h, to hate/find repugnant). Form I stative verb.
**أَكرَهُ الاِنتِظَار** = I hate waiting. The verbal noun **الاِنتِظَار** (al-intiẓār, the waiting) acts as the direct object — like a gerund in English. From root **ن-ظ-ر** (n-ẓ-r, to look/wait), Form VIII: 'to look/wait with expectation.'

Arabic uses the same verb **يُحِبّ** (yuḥibb) for both 'to love' and 'to like' — context and degree make the distinction. When followed by a definite noun (with الـ), it reads as liking: **أُحِبُّ المُوسِيقَى** = I like music. When followed by a person, it can mean love.
Compare: **أُحِبُّ المُوسِيقَى** (I like music) vs. **أُحِبُّكِ** (I love you).

**يَستَمتِع** (yastamtiʿ, he enjoys) — Form X of root **م-ت-ع** (m-t-ʿ, to enjoy/provide pleasure). Form X (**اِستَفعَلَ**) = to seek or acquire the root quality: **مَتَّعَ** (mattaʿa, to give enjoyment) → **اِستَمتَعَ** (istamtaʿa, to enjoy for oneself). Always followed by **بـ** (bi-): **اِستَمتَعَ بـ** = to enjoy [something].

**يَخشَى** (yakhshā, he fears) from root **خ-ش-ي** (kh-sh-y, to fear/dread — a stronger fear than خاف). **خَشِيَ** (khashiya, to dread/fear), **خَشيَة** (khashya, dread/reverence), **خَاشِع** (khāshiʿ, humble/reverent).
The difference: **خَافَ** = general fear; **خَشِيَ** = a deeper, reverential fear — used for fearing God as well as profound dread.

**يَأمُل** (yaʾmul, he hopes) from root **أ-م-ل** (ʾ-m-l, to hope/expect). **آمُل أَن** (āmulu an, I hope that) + subjunctive verb.
**عَلَى مَا يُرَام** (ʿalā mā yurām) = as desired/well — a fixed Arabic expression, literally 'upon what is wished.' **يُرَام** is passive present of **رَامَ** (to desire). The whole phrase means 'perfectly, all is well.'
cards.phrase: آمل أن يسير كل شيء على ما يرام.
cards.phraseRomanized: amal an yasir kull shay' 'ala ma yuram.

cards.grammarNote:
**يَقلَق** (yaqlaqu, he worries) from root **ق-ل-ق** (q-l-q, to be restless). **لَا تَقلَق** (lā taqlaqu) = don't worry — **لَا** + present tense verb = prohibition.
**بِشَأنِ** (bi-shaʾni) = regarding/about — a preposition phrase. **شَأن** (shaʾn, matter/affair/concern) from root **ش-أ-ن** (sh-ʾ-n). Compare with **بِخُصوص** (bi-khuṣūṣ, regarding) — both are common.

**يُهَدِّئ** (yuhaddiʾ, he calms/soothes) — Form II of root **ه-د-أ** (h-d-ʾ, to calm/be still). Form II doubles the middle radical = causative: **هَدَأَ** (hadaʾa, to calm down) → **هَدَّأَ** (haddaʾa, to calm someone down).
**هَدِّئ مِن نَفسِكَ** = calm yourself, literally 'calm [it] from your self' — **نَفس** (nafs, self/soul/person) is a key word: **نَفسِي** (nafsī, myself), **نَفسُهُ** (nafsuhu, himself).

**يُوَاسِي** (yuwāsī, he comforts) — Form III of root **أ-س-ي** (ʾ-s-y, to treat/console). Form III (**فَاعَلَ**) here implies a mutual or active giving of consolation: **آسَى** (āsā, to condole/comfort), **مُوَاسَاة** (muwāsāt, consolation — Form III verbal noun). Compare with **عَزَّى** (to offer condolences specifically at a death).

**يُثِير** (yuthīr, he excites/stirs) — Form IV of root **ث-و-ر** (th-w-r, to stir up/rise). Form IV (**أَفعَلَ**) = causative: **ثَارَ** (thāra, to rise up/be stirred) → **أَثَارَ** (athāra, to stir/excite). **ثَورة** (thawra, revolution), **مُثِير** (muthīr, exciting/arousing — Form IV active participle).
**حَمَاسِي** (ḥamāsī) = my enthusiasm — root **ح-م-س** (ḥ-m-s, zeal). Possessive suffix **ي** = my.

**يُزعِج** (yuzʿij, he annoys/disturbs) — Form IV of root **ز-ع-ج** (z-ʿ-j, to disturb/unsettle). Form IV = causative: **زَعِجَ** (zaʿija, to be disturbed) → **أَزعَجَ** (azʿaja, to annoy/disturb someone). **إِزعَاج** (izʿāj, annoyance — Form IV verbal noun).
The suffix **ـني** (-nī) = me: **يُزعِجني** = it annoys me. Object pronoun suffixes attach directly to the verb.

**إِحرَاج** (iḥrāj, embarrassment) — Form IV verbal noun from root **ح-ر-ج** (ḥ-r-j, to be in a tight spot/embarrassed). Form IV: **أَحرَجَ** (aḥraja, to embarrass). **مُحرِج** (muḥrij, embarrassing — Form IV active participle), **حَرَج** (ḥaraj, awkwardness/narrowness).
**أَشعُر بِالإِحرَاج** — the **بِـ** preposition is obligatory after **شَعَرَ**: شَعَرَ بِـ = to feel [noun]. The definite article on **الإِحرَاج** treats the feeling as a known state.

**غَيرَة** (ghayra, jealousy/protective zeal) from root **غ-ي-ر** (gh-y-r, to change/be jealous). In Arabic culture, **غَيرَة** can be positive (protecting what is precious) or negative (destructive jealousy). Contrast with **حَسَد** (ḥasad, envy of another's good fortune).
**قَد تُدَمِّر** (qad tudammir) = can destroy / may destroy — **قَد** before a present-tense verb expresses possibility. **عَلَاقَات** (ʿalāqāt) = relationships (sound feminine plural of **عَلَاقة**).

**يُغِيظ** (yughīẓ, it irritates/enrages) — Form IV of root **غ-ي-ظ** (gh-y-ẓ, to enrage/infuriate). **غَاظَ** (ghāẓa, to enrage), **غَيظ** (ghayẓ, rage/irritation). The phrase uses **يُغِيظُني** — Form IV active + suffix **ـني** (me).
Note: the card word **يُهَيِّج** (yuhayij) is Form II of **ه-ي-ج** (h-y-j, to agitate/excite), while the example uses the synonym **يُغِيظ** — both are natural Arabic ways to say 'irritate.'

**يُغضِب** (yughḍib, he angers/makes angry) — Form IV of root **غ-ض-ب** (gh-ḍ-b, to be angry). Form IV = causative: **غَضِبَ** (ghaḍiba, to become angry) → **أَغضَبَ** (aghḍaba, to make angry). **أَغضَبَني** (aghḍabanī) = angered me — note **ـني** suffix.
Compare the family: **غَضَب** (noun: anger), **غَضبَان** (adj: furious — **فَعلَان** emotional pattern), **غَاضِب** (adj: angry — **فَاعِل**), **يُغضِب** (verb: to anger).

**يُبهِج** (yubhij, he delights) — Form IV of root **ب-ه-ج** (b-h-j, to be radiant/joyful). Form IV = causative: **بَهِجَ** (bahija, to be joyful/radiant) → **أَبهَجَ** (abhaja, to delight/gladden). **بَهجَة** (bahja, joy/beauty — noun), **بَهِيج** (bahīj, delightful — **فَعِيل** adjective).
**أَبهَجَت** (abhajat) — past tense feminine, because **الموسيقى** (al-mūsīqā) is grammatically feminine.

**يَبتَسِم** (yabtasim, he smiles) — Form VIII of root **ب-س-م** (b-s-m, to smile). Form VIII (**اِفتَعَلَ**) here intensifies the action slightly: **بَسَمَ** (basama, to smile gently) → **اِبتَسَمَ** (ibtasama, to smile). **اِبتِسَامة** (ibtisāma, a smile — Form VIII verbal noun).
**دَائِماً** (dāʾiman, always) — accusative adverb from **دَوَام** (dawām, continuity), root **د-و-م** (d-w-m, to be permanent).

**يَضحَك** (yaḍḥak, he laughs) from root **ض-ح-ك** (ḍ-ḥ-k, to laugh). **ضَحِكَ** (ḍaḥika, to laugh — Form I), **ضَحكَة** (ḍaḥka, a laugh), **ضَحَّاك** (ḍaḥḥāk, someone who laughs a lot — **فَعَّال** intensive). Note: **ضَحِكَ على** (ḍaḥika ʿalā) = to laugh *at* someone (mocking); **ضَحِكَ مع** = to laugh *with*.
**ضَحِكنَا** (ḍaḥiknā) = we laughed — the suffix **ـنَا** (-nā) = we (past tense).

**يَبكِي** (yabkī, he cries) from root **ب-ك-ي** (b-k-y, to cry/weep). Defective verb — the final **ي** affects conjugation: present **يَبكِي**, past **بَكَى** (bakā). **بُكَاء** (bukāʾ, crying/weeping — verbal noun), **بَاكٍ** (bākin, one who cries — **فَاعِل** with dropped **ي**).
The sun letter **ط** in **الطِّفل** (aṭ-ṭifl, the child) causes assimilation. **طِفل** has broken plural **أَطفَال** (aṭfāl).

**يَتَنَهَّد** (yatanhahhad, he sighs) — Form V of root **ن-ه-د** (n-h-d, to heave/sigh). Form V (**تَفَعَّلَ**) often implies a deliberate or repeated action: **تَنَهَّدَ** (tanahhadda, to sigh/heave a sigh). **تَنَهُّد** (tanahud, a sigh — verbal noun).
**اِرتِيَاحاً** (irtiyāḥan) = with relief — indefinite accusative form used as an adverbial of manner (ḥāl). This structure is common: verb + noun in accusative to describe how something was done.

**يُعَبِّس وَجهَهُ** (yuʿabbisu wajhahu, he frowns) — Form II of root **ع-ب-س** (ʿ-b-s, to frown/scowl). Form II (**فَعَّلَ**) intensifies: **عَبَسَ** (ʿabasa, to frown) → **عَبَّسَ** (ʿabbasa, to scowl/frown deeply). **وَجه** (wajh, face) with the possessive suffix **ـهُ** (hu, his).
The Quranic chapter عَبَسَ (Surah 80) is named after this very root — the Prophet frowned at a blind man.
cards.englishPhrase: He frowned when he heard the news.

cards.grammarNote:
**يَصرُخ** (yaṣrukh, he screams) from root **ص-ر-خ** (ṣ-r-kh, to scream/cry out). **صَرَخَ** (ṣarakha, to scream), **صُرَاخ** (ṣurākh, screaming — verbal noun), **صَرخَة** (ṣarkha, a scream). Sun letter **ص** causes assimilation of ال.
**لَا تَصرُخ** = don't shout — prohibition: **لَا** + present tense (jussive form). The jussive of **يَصرُخ** is the same form here. MSA and colloquial both use this construction for commands.

**يَستَرخِي** (yastarkhi, he relaxes) — Form X of root **ر-خ-و/ي** (r-kh-w, to be loose). Form X = to become/seek a state: **اِسترَخَى** (istarkha, to relax/go loose). Related: **رَخو** (rakhw, loose/soft), **اِرتِخَاء** (irtikhaʾ, relaxation/slackness).
**لَا دَاعِيَ لِـ** (lā dāʿiya li-, there is no need for) — a fixed construction: **دَاعٍ** (dāʿin, reason/motive — **فَاعِل** of root **د-ع-و**, to call/invite).

**غَاضِب** (ghāḍib, angry) — **فَاعِل** active participle from root **غ-ض-ب** (gh-ḍ-b, to be angry). Describes the current state of being angry: **أَنَا غَاضِب** = I am (currently) angry. Feminine: **غَاضِبة** (ghāḍiba).
Contrast: **غَضبَان** (ghaḍbān, furious — **فَعلَان** pattern) implies a stronger, more agitated state. **غَضبَانة** (ghaḍbāna, furious, f.) — this pattern is especially common in colloquial Arabic for emotional states.

**غَضبَان** (ghaḍbān, furious/mad) — **فَعلَان** pattern from root **غ-ض-ب** (gh-ḍ-b, to be angry). The **فَعلَان** pattern describes an intense emotional or physical state: **عَطشَان** (ʿaṭshān, thirsty), **جَوعَان** (jawʿān, hungry), **تَعبَان** (taʿbān, tired — colloquial), **حَزنَان** (ḥaznān, sad).
Feminine: **غَضبَى** (ghaḍbā) in MSA, **غَضبَانة** (ghaḍbāna) in colloquial. This pattern is much more common in spoken Arabic than in formal writing.

**خَائِف** (khāʾif, afraid) — **فَاعِل** active participle from root **خ-و-ف** (kh-w-f, to fear). Describes a current state: **أَنَا خَائِف** = I am afraid. Feminine: **خَائِفة** (khāʾifa).
**كَانَ الطِّفلُ خَائِفاً** — **كَانَ** (kāna) + adjective in the accusative (**خَائِفاً** with tanwin **ـاً**). This is the standard structure for past states: كان + predicate accusative. Note sun letter **ط** in **الطِّفل**: *aṭ-ṭifl*.

**قَلِق** (qaliq, anxious/restless) — **فَعِل** stative adjective from root **ق-ل-ق** (q-l-q, to be restless). Note the doubled root letters (q-l-q) — a reduplicated root. **قَلَق** (qalaq, anxiety — noun), **قَلِقَ** (qaliqa, to be anxious).
Feminine: **قَلِقة** (qaliqa). The preposition **بِشَأنِ** (bi-shaʾni, regarding/about) is very common in MSA: **قَلِق بِشَأنِ** = anxious about. More colloquial: **خَايِف عَلَى** (afraid for).

**مُتَوَتِّر** (mutawattir, nervous/tense) — Form V active participle from root **و-ت-ر** (w-t-r, to tighten/string). Form V (**تَفَعَّلَ**): **وَتَّرَ** (wattara, to tighten/make taut) → **تَوَتَّرَ** (tawattara, to become tense). **تَوَتُّر** (tawattur, tension/nervousness — Form V verbal noun).
Feminine: **مُتَوَتِّرة** (mutawattira). Colloquially, Egyptians often say **مُتوَتِّر** or **خَايِف** for nervous.

**مُتَحَمِّس** (mutaḥammis, excited/enthusiastic) — Form V active participle from root **ح-م-س** (ḥ-m-s, to be zealous). Form V: **حَمَّسَ** (ḥammasa, to enthuse someone) → **تَحَمَّسَ** (taḥammasa, to become enthusiastic). **حَمَاس** (ḥamās, enthusiasm/zeal — also the name of the Palestinian movement).
**لِلرِّحلة** (lir-riḥla) = for the trip — **لِـ** (li-, for) + sun letter **ر** causes assimilation: *lir-riḥla*.

**مُبهِج** (mubhij, joyful/delightful) — Form IV active participle from root **ب-ه-ج** (b-h-j, to be radiant). Note the phrase uses **مَرِحة** (mariḥa, cheerful/merry) — **فَعِل** adjective pattern from root **م-ر-ح** (m-r-ḥ, to be jolly). Both describe cheerfulness but differ slightly: **مُبهِج** = causing delight (active), **مَرِح** = being merry (stative).
Feminine agreement: **مَرِحة** agrees with **هي** (she).

**فَخُور** (fakhūr, proud) — **فَعُول** intensive adjective pattern from root **ف-خ-ر** (f-kh-r, to be proud). **فَعُول** describes an enduring quality or strong disposition: **صَبُور** (patient), **شَكُور** (grateful), **غَيُور** (jealous/zealous). **فَخر** (fakhr, pride — noun), **مَفخَرة** (mafkhara, source of pride).
**فَخُور بِكَ** = proud of you — the **بِـ** preposition follows **فَخُور**: be proud of [someone].

**رَاضٍ** (rāḍin, content/satisfied) — **فَاعِل** active participle of root **ر-ض-ي** (r-ḍ-y, to be content/approve). The final **ي** drops before case endings, giving the indefinite form **رَاضٍ** (with tanwin). Definite: **الرَّاضِي** (ar-rāḍī). Feminine: **رَاضِيَة** (rāḍiya).
**رَضِيَ عَن** = to be content with [something]. **رِضَا** (riḍā, satisfaction/approval — also a personal name) from the same root.

**هَادِئ** (hādiʾ, calm/quiet) — **فَاعِل** active participle from root **ه-د-أ** (h-d-ʾ, to be still/calm). Feminine: **هَادِئة** (hādiʾa). **هُدُوء** (hudūʾ, calmness — verbal noun), **تَهدِئة** (tahdiʾa, calming/soothing — Form II verbal noun).
**اِبقَ هَادِئاً** = stay calm — imperative **اِبقَ** (ibqa, stay/remain) from root **ب-ق-ي** (b-q-y) + **هَادِئاً** in accusative as a state complement (ḥāl). This structure means 'remain in the state of being calm.'

**مَضغُوط** (maḍghūṭ, stressed/under pressure) — passive participle **مَفعُول** from root **ض-غ-ط** (ḍ-gh-ṭ, to press/squeeze). Literally 'pressed upon/squeezed.' **ضَغط** (ḍaghṭ, pressure), **ضَغط الدَّم** (blood pressure), **ضَغط العَمَل** (work pressure).
In MSA, **مُجهَد** (mujhad, exhausted) or **تَحتَ ضَغط** (under pressure) are also used. **مَضغُوط** is especially common in Levantine and Gulf colloquial Arabic for 'stressed out.'

**مَغلُوب عَلَى أَمرِهِ** (maghūb ʿalā amrihi) = overwhelmed, literally 'overcome in his matter.' **مَغلُوب** (maghūb, overcome — passive participle of root **غ-ل-ب**, to overcome/defeat). **أَمر** (amr, matter/affair/command). The possessive suffix **ـهِ** refers to the subject.
**بِسَبَب** (bi-sabab, because of/due to) — preposition + noun: **سَبَب** (sabab, reason/cause) from root **س-ب-ب** (s-b-b). Plural: **أَسبَاب** (asbāb, reasons).
cards.englishPhrase: I feel overwhelmed by the work.
cards.phrase: أشعر أنني مغلوب على أمري بسبب العمل.
cards.phraseRomanized: ash'ur annani maghlub 'ala amri bi-sabab al-'amal.

cards.grammarNote:
**نَشِيط** (nashīṭ, energetic/active) — **فَعِيل** adjective pattern from root **ن-ش-ط** (n-sh-ṭ, to be active/vigorous). **نَشَاط** (nashāṭ, activity/energy — verbal noun), **أَنشِطة** (anshiṭa, activities — broken plural **أَفعِلة**).
Feminine agreement: **نَشِيطَة** (nashīṭa) agrees with **هي** (she). The predicate adjective takes no definite article: **هي نَشِيطَة** = she is energetic (equational sentence, no verb 'to be').

**مُتعَب** (mutʿab, tired) — passive participle **مُفعَل** from root **ت-ع-ب** (t-ʿ-b, to be tired). **أَتعَبَ** (atʿaba, to tire someone out — Form IV), **مُتعَب** = 'made tired/worn out.' **تَعَب** (taʿab, tiredness — noun), **تَعبَان** (taʿbān, tired — colloquial **فَعلَان** pattern, very common in spoken Arabic).
In MSA prefer **مُتعَب**; colloquially **تَعبَان** is more natural: أنا تعبان = I'm tired.

**مُنهَك** (munhak, exhausted) — passive participle from root **ن-ه-ك** (n-h-k, to exhaust/wear down). **أَنهَكَ** (anhaka, to exhaust — Form IV), **نُهُوك** (nuhūk, exhaustion — verbal noun). The **مُفعَل** pattern = 'made to be' in the root state: **مُنهَك** = 'made completely worn down.'
Distinguish degrees: **مُتعَب** (tired) < **مُنهَك** (exhausted) < **مُنهَك تَمَاماً** (completely drained).

**فُضُولِيّ** (fuḍūlī, curious/nosy) — nisba adjective from **فُضُول** (fuḍūl, curiosity/nosiness). Root **ف-ض-ل** (f-ḍ-l, excess/virtue): **فُضُول** originally meant 'excess matters / things beyond one's need,' hence nosiness or curiosity. **فَضِيلة** (faḍīla, virtue — same root), **أَفضَل** (afḍal, better/best).
Note: **فُضُولِيّ** can mean 'nosy' (peeking into others' affairs) as well as 'curious' — context determines the tone. A more neutral word is **مُتَفَضِّل** or **فَضُولِيّ بِطَبعِهِ** (naturally curious).
